---
pagetitle: "EpiAware, composable tools for infectious disease modelling"
description: "A composable Julia ecosystem for infectious disease modelling."
toc: false
page-layout: custom
section-divs: false
hide-description: true
listing:
  - id: news-listing
    contents: news/posts
    max-items: 3
    sort: "date desc"
    fields: [date, title, description]
---

:::::: {.side-space}

<!-- Header -->
::: {.logo-header}

{{< include _includes/header.qmd >}}

:::
<!-- Header End Here -->

<!-- What is EpiAware Section -->
::: {.what-is-turing-grid .section-start-space .section-end-space}

::: {.feature-text-column}

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-composable"></span>
```

##### Composable

:::

Small, interchangeable parts combine into one joint model. Swap a part to test an assumption, keeping a single rigorous model rather than chaining separate ones.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-uncertainty"></span>
```

##### Uncertainty-aware

:::

Uncertainty propagated correctly from the priors through infection dynamics and observation.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-response"></span>
```

##### Built for response

:::

Nowcasting, forecasting, and scenario modelling from one model definition, with reporting delays and ascertainment built in.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-julia"></span>
```

##### Built on Julia

:::

Built on the work of people who specialise in each piece, rather than as a walled garden. Inference comes from Turing.jl, dynamics from SciML, and automatic differentiation from Mooncake and Enzyme.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-approaches"></span>
```

##### Multiple approaches

:::

Aims to support more than one way of composing infectious disease models, kept interoperable. See the [approaches](approaches/index.qmd) we are exploring.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-llm"></span>
```

##### LLM-assisted model building

:::

Named, composable parts give large language models a clear vocabulary to assemble and adapt models from, lowering the barrier to a first analysis.

:::

:::

::: {.code-example-column}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

::: 

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>composed_delays.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposedDistributions, Distributions

# BDBV-inspired delay tree: parallel pathways
# with Sequential, Resolve, and Compete nested together
tree = compose((
    clinical = sequential(
        :onset_admit => Gamma(1.2, 3.0),
        :admit_resolve => resolve(
            :death => (Gamma(2.0, 3.5), 0.3),
            :discharge => (Gamma(1.0, 8.0), 0.7))),
    surveillance = sequential(
        :onset_notif => Gamma(0.7, 20.0),
        :notif_compete => compete(
            :confirmed => Gamma(2.0, 1.0),
            :lost => Gamma(5.0, 0.5))),
))

# Inspect tree, params, and default priors
event_tree(tree)
params_table(tree)
priors = build_priors(params_table(tree))

# Draw a synthetic case, or evaluate a real one's likelihood
rand(tree)
logpdf(tree, ans)
```

:::
:::

:::
<!-- What is EpiAware Section Ends Here -->


<!-- Why composable Section -->
::: {#why-composable .section-start-space}

### Why composable?
<p>Evidence for infectious disease policy has to be timely, rigorous, and collaborative. Today's approaches force a choice between them, and composability may let us avoid it.</p>

:::

::: {.section-end-space}
- **Passing results from one model to the next loses information.** Each model hands on a summary rather than the underlying evidence, and quietly carries over the assumptions made before it. By the time an answer reaches the person making a decision, nobody can check what that cost.
- **Building one model that does everything is more reliable, but it does not survive the next outbreak.** The models built for COVID-19 could not be adapted for mpox, so new ones were written from scratch, and had to make do with a single source of data because answers were needed quickly.
- **We keep rebuilding the same things.** At least seven separate software packages estimate how fast a disease is spreading, and none of them share any parts, even where the same people wrote them. What gets reused is the whole model, never the ideas inside it.
- **Composable models could give us both.** Build a model from interchangeable parts and it can be as reliable as an all-in-one model while still being quick to adapt. A specialist can contribute the one part they know well without understanding the rest, and when two teams disagree they can see whether it is a real scientific difference or just a difference in the code.

[Read the full argument](news/posts/hello-epiaware.qmd)
:::
<!-- Why composable Section Ends Here -->


<!-- Core Packages Section -->
:::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#core-packages-header-content}

### Core Packages
<p>The EpiAware ecosystem is built on a foundation of powerful, composable packages.</p>

:::

```{=html}
<a href="packages/index.qmd" class="button desktop-only-button">Explore Ecosystem &rarr;</a>
```

::::

::::: {.core-packages-grid .section-end-space}

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ComposableTuringIDModels.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](https://composableturingidmodels.epiaware.org/dev/){.panel-title}
<p>Assemble infectious disease models from interchangeable parts as a single Turing model.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [CensoredDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/){.panel-title}
<p>Handle the censoring and truncation biases common in epidemiological delay data.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ComposedDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](https://composeddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/){.panel-title}
<p>A grammar for composing distributions into event trees.</p>

:::

::: {.package-card .box #core-card}

##### [ModifiedDistributions.jl <i class="bi bi-box-arrow-up-right" style="font-size: 0.4em;"></i>](https://modifieddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/){.panel-title}
<p>Rescale, weight, or modify distributions, keeping the full distribution interface.</p>

:::

:::::

::: {.mobile-only-button-container}
```{=html}
<a href="packages/index.qmd" class="button btn">Explore Ecosystem &rarr;</a>
```
:::
<!-- Core Packages Section Ends Here -->




<!-- News & Updates Section -->
:::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#news}

### News & Updates
<p>Read the latest from the EpiAware team.</p>

:::

```{=html}
<a href="news/index.qmd" class="button desktop-only-button">View more &rarr;</a>
```
::::

::: {#news-listing}
:::

::: {.mobile-only-button-container}
```{=html}
<a href="news/index.qmd" class="button">View more &rarr;</a>
```
:::
<!-- News & Updates Section Ends Here -->


<!-- Built on Julia Section -->
::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#built-on-julia}

### Built on Julia
<p>We build in Julia for its type system, multiple dispatch, and automatic differentiation, on top of the scientific infrastructure.</p>

:::

:::

::: {.feature-grid .foundations .section-end-space}
::: {.feature-card}
### [SciML](https://sciml.ai)
Differential equations, sensitivity analysis, and scientific machine learning.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org)
Probabilistic programming and Bayesian inference.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl)
The shared vocabulary of probability distributions our packages extend.
:::
:::

::: {.text-center .section-end-space}
New to Julia? Start with the [Using Julia](using-julia.qmd) guide.
:::

<!-- Background Section -->
::: {.section-header .section-start-space}

::: {#background}

### Background
<p>From R to Julia, the story behind EpiAware.</p>

:::

:::

::: {.section-end-space}
In R we built the [epinowcast](community.qmd) ecosystem and packages such as [EpiNow2](https://github.com/epiforecasts/EpiNow2) and [scoringutils](https://github.com/epiforecasts/scoringutils), now used by academics and public health teams internationally.
EpiAware is the Julia equivalent, a domain-focused ecosystem in the mould of SciML or Turing.jl, with community infrastructure like [rOpenSci](https://ropensci.org) and the domain focus of [SpeedyWeather.jl](https://github.com/SpeedyWeather/SpeedyWeather.jl).

We are at an early stage and looking for collaborators, and [here is how to get involved](get-involved.qmd).
:::

::::::
